items.area: Named economic buyer at Dubai management / Australia HQ
items.quote: the management team above Kerolos... and ultimately the global CFO / University of Wollongong Australia holds budget authority

metrics.narrative: Zycus presented generic efficiency claims in the 16 Jan 2026 discovery/demo call (roughly 20% shorter sourcing cycles, 30% faster contract review, 60% less manual AP/invoice work), but these are vendor-asserted, not buyer-validated. Kerolos Abdelmalk is building his own internal business case with cost-saving and risk-reduction numbers to present to Dubai management ('I will build a business case... telling them in our procurement process, we have risk in one, two, 3, 4, and we can cover this risk through this system, and the cost of this system is x' - Kerolos Abdelmalk, 16 Jan 2026 call), but no quantified buyer-side ROI figure has been confirmed as of the 4 Jun 2026 Next Step update.

champion.narrative: Kerolos Abdelmalk self-initiated the relationship after meeting a Zycus business development representative at a CPO event, drove two substantive discovery/scoping calls, requested detailed per-module pricing to strengthen his internal case, and submitted a light RFP on 22 May 2026 - real advocacy signals for a champion. However, there is no direct evidence he is actively selling Zycus internally to Dubai management or the Australia parent versus simply running a compliant multi-vendor evaluation, and his own rep-logged confidence sits at only 10% (Next_Step__c, as of 2 Jul 2026), so he is marked developing rather than confirmed. Aygul Gurbanova (Senior Procurement Officer) participated in both calls as a secondary, less-vocal champion.

competition.narrative: Kerolos confirmed on the 30 Jan 2026 call that UOWD is running a multi-vendor evaluation ('I met with a lot of providers who can automate this'), and Next_Step_History__c confirms 'a light RFP submitted' on 22 May 2026 alongside other providers - but no competitor has ever been named in any source. Salesforce's Competitors__c field is blank and no call or note surfaces a rival by name.

identify_pain.narrative: Kerolos Abdelmalk described the pain in detail on 16 Jan 2026: contract lifecycle is fully manual via email/PDF/Excel across 87-100 contracts per year, with 'lack of payment tracking, payment term tracking... whenever it's a web to any change... we have no track', plus 30-50 weekly RFQs run with zero system tracking. Oracle Fusion (the ERP purchased by the Australia parent) has no contract module because the parent did not buy one for the smaller Dubai campus, leaving a structural gap Kerolos is trying to close.

paper_process.narrative: No formal Zycus contracting artifact (MSA / Order Form / SOW) has entered play. References to an 'SOW' shared on 1 May 2026 and an 'SOW/RFP' extension granted on 14 May 2026 describe the buyer's own tender/RFP scope document, not a post-selection Zycus contracting SOW. At Formal Evaluation stage with no vendor selection confirmed, contracting mechanics have not started.

economic_buyer.narrative: CRM MEDDPICC 2.0 names Kerolos Abdelmalk as economic buyer, but this is contradicted by both calls: Kerolos states the project needs Dubai management approval and then escalation to the Australia HQ parent for budget release ('Once the management approve, they will share it to the relevant stakeholder will give me budget' - Kerolos Abdelmalk, 30 Jan 2026 call). The CRM's own budget-owner field confirms 'the management team above Kerolos... and ultimately the global University of Wollongong Australia holds budget authority.' No one from Dubai management or the Australia parent has ever joined a Zycus call; the only indirect executive signal is that the global CFO (visited January 2026) 'encouraged automation across processes' during a January 2026 visit. Access to power remains entirely indirect via Kerolos.

decision_process.narrative: A clear 4-step process was defined on 30 Jan 2026 (collect vendor proposals by end-Feb 2026, build an internal business case, get Dubai management approval, then escalate to the relevant stakeholder for budget), but it has run roughly 4-5 months behind that original schedule. Next_Step_History__c shows the project was 'delayed by the war' and only 'heading back on track' by 1 May 2026, with a light RFP not submitted until 22 May 2026, and buyer evaluation feedback ('expect feedback by end June') still unconfirmed as of 9 Jul 2026 - roughly 9 days past that self-set deadline.

decision_criteria.narrative: Five criteria are clearly established and corroborated across both calls and the CRM: (1) seamless Oracle Fusion integration is a hard requirement so the AP team can match invoices/payments against contract milestones; (2) an end-to-end RFQ-to-contract-to-payment workflow, not just a repository; (3) AI-assisted contract clause detection/red-flagging (Kerolos asked directly whether the system 'will suggest some edits and red flag whatever out of our terms and condition', 16 Jan 2026 call); (4) transparent, itemised per-module pricing to support a phased business case ('I prefer to receive the quotation from your side of your proposal, giving separate prices, not lump sum' - Kerolos, 30 Jan 2026 call).

items.summary: Mohamad Al Hakim follows up directly, noting he's already met Kerolos and Aygul in a prior role, confirms he's been briefed on the UOWD/Zycus history, and asks Kerolos to share time slots to reconnect — a reply is now pending from UOWD.

day_summary.overall: After a rep change on the Zycus side, VP Carl Kimball re-introduced UOWD to the new Middle East RVP, Mohamad Al Hakim, who turns out to already know Kerolos and Aygul from a previous role. Hakim has picked up the account history and is now asking Kerolos to share availability for a re-engagement meeting - so the ball is in UOWD's court to schedule, and the deal is essentially restarting relationship-building after the account handoff.

cro_panel.intro: UOWD sits in a real RFP evaluation round with well-documented pain and a genuinely self-initiated champion, but the actual economic buyer (Dubai management / Australia HQ finance) has never engaged, no competitor is known despite a live multi-vendor field, and the buyer's own end-June 2026 feedback deadline has already passed without confirmation; keeping the win read modest despite the rich early discovery

flags: Surface the competitive shortlist and build the wedge now. At Formal Evaluation in a multi-vendor RFP (confirmed 22 May 'light RFP submitted') with unknown competition, Zycus is flying blind. Not knowing who we are up against or how we compare on fit/cost is a critical gap 35 days from close. Next move: on the re-engagement call, ask Kerolos candidly 'who else are you evaluating, and how do we stack up on Oracle integration and contract-risk fit versus them?' Use the answer to sharpen the wedge: emphasize Zycus's Oracle Fusion customer base ('dozens of multibillion-dollar customers' per Dan 30 Jan), the contract lifecycle + AI-assisted redlining fit against his stated pain (manual contracts = high risk of late payment and missed renewals), and the multi-campus expansion potential (Dubai success → Malaysia/Hong Kong/Australia/India rollout, which justifies the investment and aligns with the global CFO's automation mandate). If Kerolos names a lower-cost competitor, the countermove is ROI and risk mitigation (lower upfront cost but higher operational risk and manual toil) plus the Australia parent precedent (Oracle ERP was a cross-campus enterprise buy, CLM/sourcing should be too).

No champion: Kerolos is friendly but not advocating internally; no evidence of EB introduction, competitive intelligence sharing, or proactive deal-driving. 10% confidence rating and 27-day silence suggest deal is passive or deprioritized.

Buyer silent 27 days past expected feedback deadline: Next Step 4 Jun expected end-June feedback; today is 29 Jun with no buyer contact since 2 Jun. Rep outreach 4 Jun awaiting reply. Deal may be stalled or deprioritized.

Close date 31 Jul (32 days) not credible; stage is Formal Evaluation, no business-case approval, no EB engagement, no contract/legal/InfoSec process started; realistic timeline from approval to signature is 60-90 days, suggesting Q3 or Q4 2026 close more likely

Extract competitive intelligence and position the wedge before the RFP closes. Zero competitors named 24 days from close despite a confirmed multi-vendor RFP — we are flying blind. Kerolos's five decision criteria (Oracle integration, S2C workflow, AI clause detection, per-module pricing, user-count fit) suggest he is comparing integrated S2C platforms, likely Ivalua, Coupa CLM, SAP Ariba, GEP, or regional vendors who can undercut Zycus on price. Dan flagged UOWD is 'towards the smaller end' and may find Zycus expensive; Kerolos stated 'if budget is constrained my priority is contracts' — if a rival offers standalone CLM at $80k–100k vs. Zycus $150k–200k bundled, we lose. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos and ask directly: who else is in the RFP, what are they proposing, how does Zycus compare? Then position our wedge (Merlin AI, best-in-class CLM, Oracle customer precedent with dozens of blue-chip universities) against the specific shortlist. If Kerolos won't share, that signals weak championship and we should prepare for loss or no-decision.

Identify and engage the true Dubai management and/or the University of Wollongong Australia finance authority who controls budget approval - since Kerolos, while a strong process champion, has no budget authority himself.
Close the still-open SOW confirm/decline loop from 1 May 2026 explicitly -- it is the qualification gate that precedes any real RFP evaluation outcome.

Get a named contact above Kerolos (Dubai management or the Australia HQ budget owner) onto a call; at $135k this is a realistic ask, and until it happens Zycus has zero direct signal on approval odds.

deal_risk.text: Close date of 31 Jul 2026 (22 days away) is not credible at Formal Evaluation stage with no confirmed business case approval, no EB engagement, and no contracting/legal motion started; the rep's own Next Step logs a 10% confidence rating.

Deal size (~$135k ACV, ~7 power users) sits at what the Zycus regional VP called 'towards the smaller end' of target deal size in the 30 Jan 2026 call, and Zycus's no-single-module policy could disadvantage it if UOWD needs a lower-cost, modular-only option under budget constraints.

win_position.text: Kerolos Abdelmalk self-initiated the engagement after meeting a Zycus rep at a CPO event and has driven two deep discovery/scoping calls (16 Jan 2026 and 30 Jan 2026) plus a light RFP submission (22 May 2026), evidencing genuine process ownership.

Pain is thoroughly documented and buyer-articulated: fully manual, email-based contract lifecycle across 87-100 contracts/year and 30-50 weekly RFQs with no renewal alerts or audit trail (Avoma call, 16 Jan 2026).

No economic buyer has ever joined a Zycus call; budget authority sits with Dubai management and ultimately the Australia HQ / global CFO (per CRM MEDDPICC 2.0 budget-owner field), and neither has engaged directly as of 9 Jul 2026.

The evaluation-feedback deadline the buyer set for end-June 2026 (Next_Step_History__c, 4 Jun 2026) is now roughly 9 days overdue with no confirmation of business-case submission, approval, or vendor selection.
Zero competitors are named despite a confirmed live multi-vendor RFP ('a light RFP submitted', 22 May 2026); Zycus has no visibility into its standing in the field.

deal_momentum.text: Since the two January 2026 discovery calls, the deal has run entirely by email/RFP correspondence for roughly 5.5 months with no further recorded call, consistent with a partner-led, low-touch APAC motion.

A genuine two-way buyer touch was logged on 2 Jun 2026 (incoming email reply, ~5 weeks ago), and the rep followed up with a status/introduction email on 2 Jul 2026 (~1 week ago), so the thread remains open even if slow.
The rep's 2 Jul 2026 outreach (status update plus introducing the incoming deal owner) is a one-way send awaiting reply as of 9 Jul 2026; no buyer response has been logged yet.

customer_commitment.text: Kerolos committed, unprompted, to build and submit an internal business case incorporating Zycus's per-module pricing and ROI metrics (Avoma call, 30 Jan 2026).

That commitment has not translated into a confirmed budget approval; the process has already slipped roughly 4-5 months past the original end-Feb/March 2026 target, attributed in the Next Step to 'the war' delaying the project (Next_Step_History__c, 1 May 2026).

No champion; Kerolos friendly but not advocating internally, no EB introduction, no competitive intelligence shared, 10% confidence rating and 27-day buyer silence suggest deal passive or deprioritized, risks silent 'no' or indefinite deferral

Map and engage the economic buyer before the shortlist locks. Kerolos is Decision Maker but holds no budget authority — Dubai management and the Australia parent (likely the Dubai CFO and an Australia finance/procurement lead) are the two-tier EB gate, and both are unnamed and uninvolved 147 days in. At $150k–200k annual spend the EB must validate the ROI and timeline. Without EB sponsorship Kerolos cannot defend Zycus vs. a lower-cost rival in the business case, and the deal risks a silent no-decision or indefinite deferral. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos, ask him to name the Dubai approver and the Australia budget holder, then secure an EB call to validate the pain, confirm the investment is defensible, and test whether the March approval timeline is still live.

Co-author the quantified business case and convert Kerolos from passive contact to active champion. Kerolos articulated qualitative pain comprehensively but has not quantified the ROI — no dollar savings, FTE reduction, or cycle-time improvement to justify $150k–200k to the Dubai CFO and Australia parent. Dan offered typical savings percentages (20% sourcing cycle reduction, 30% faster contract review, 15% supplier risk reduction) but Kerolos has not circled back, and the 10% confidence rating plus 35-day buyer silence suggest the business case is stalled or deprioritized. A passive contact becomes a champion when they co-own the business case and sell it internally. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos, map his 87–100 annual contracts and 30–40 weekly RFQs to Zycus benchmarks, translate qualitative pain into defendable ROI numbers, and co-author the business-case narrative he presents to Dubai + Australia. That co-authorship builds championship and keeps Zycus competitive in the RFP.

value.flag: Surface the competitive shortlist and build the wedge now. At Formal Evaluation in a multi-vendor RFP (confirmed 22 May 'light RFP submitted') with unknown competition, Zycus is flying blind. Not knowing who we are up against or how we compare on fit/cost is a critical gap 35 days from close. Next move: on the re-engagement call, ask Kerolos candidly 'who else are you evaluating, and how do we stack up on Oracle integration and contract-risk fit versus them?' Use the answer to sharpen the wedge: emphasize Zycus's Oracle Fusion customer base ('dozens of multibillion-dollar customers' per Dan 30 Jan), the contract lifecycle + AI-assisted redlining fit against his stated pain (manual contracts = high risk of late payment and missed renewals), and the multi-campus expansion potential (Dubai success → Malaysia/Hong Kong/Australia/India rollout, which justifies the investment and aligns with the global CFO's automation mandate). If Kerolos names a lower-cost competitor, the countermove is ROI and risk mitigation (lower upfront cost but higher operational risk and manual toil) plus the Australia parent precedent (Oracle ERP was a cross-campus enterprise buy, CLM/sourcing should be too).

value.flag: Extract competitive intelligence and position the wedge before the RFP closes. Zero competitors named 24 days from close despite a confirmed multi-vendor RFP — we are flying blind. Kerolos's five decision criteria (Oracle integration, S2C workflow, AI clause detection, per-module pricing, user-count fit) suggest he is comparing integrated S2C platforms, likely Ivalua, Coupa CLM, SAP Ariba, GEP, or regional vendors who can undercut Zycus on price. Dan flagged UOWD is 'towards the smaller end' and may find Zycus expensive; Kerolos stated 'if budget is constrained my priority is contracts' — if a rival offers standalone CLM at $80k–100k vs. Zycus $150k–200k bundled, we lose. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos and ask directly: who else is in the RFP, what are they proposing, how does Zycus compare? Then position our wedge (Merlin AI, best-in-class CLM, Oracle customer precedent with dozens of blue-chip universities) against the specific shortlist. If Kerolos won't share, that signals weak championship and we should prepare for loss or no-decision.

value.flag: Co-author the quantified business case and convert Kerolos from passive contact to active champion. Kerolos articulated qualitative pain comprehensively but has not quantified the ROI — no dollar savings, FTE reduction, or cycle-time improvement to justify $150k–200k to the Dubai CFO and Australia parent. Dan offered typical savings percentages (20% sourcing cycle reduction, 30% faster contract review, 15% supplier risk reduction) but Kerolos has not circled back, and the 10% confidence rating plus 35-day buyer silence suggest the business case is stalled or deprioritized. A passive contact becomes a champion when they co-own the business case and sell it internally. The next move is to re-engage Kerolos, map his 87–100 annual contracts and 30–40 weekly RFQs to Zycus benchmarks, translate qualitative pain into defendable ROI numbers, and co-author the business-case narrative he presents to Dubai + Australia. That co-authorship builds championship and keeps Zycus competitive in the RFP.
